Volcanic Eruptions and Resulting Deaths¶

Introduction¶

Volcanoes have been erupting ever since the earth's creation, and humnaity has only been around for a fraction of that time to witness these eruptions. Volcanic eruptions tend to be very dangerous and cause mass distruction, and I will use the significant-volcanic-eruption-database.csv from opendatasoft and thats also available from NOAA to investigate the 835 recorded volcanic eruptions. This dataset only includes information about the volcanic eruptions themselves. Sadly, it doesn't include data that says whether or not the area the volcano erupted in was populated or not.

In this notebook I will look into questions about deaths that result from volcanoes and what makes volcanoes destructive. What volcano type typically has the highest explosivity index? Did more deaths occur from volcanoes in the past then they do now? Finally, is it possible predict the range of deaths that would occur from a volcanic explosion, using the data about the volcanic eruption?


Data Explanation and Exploration¶

In this section I will load the data and explain what the dataset is. Then I will wrangle the data, a lot of the data within the original csv is tidy and ready for exploration, but some minor adjustments need to be made. Afterwards, I will begin to explore the data with visualizations and summary tables/statistics. These explorations may help me to start to answer the research questions above.

In [1]:
# importing the necessary libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import altair as alt
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt


alt.data_transformers.enable("vegafusion")


from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, plot_tree

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification


# Tools to split the dataset and validate the model
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score

# Tools to validate the regression results using the "testing" portion
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score

# Tools to validate classification results
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, classification_report, RocCurveDisplay


data = pd.read_csv("../Data/significant-volcanic-eruption-database.csv", delimiter=";")
data

memory usage: 228.4+ KB

Looking at column list above, the data has 35 columns and 835 rows. Each row represents a single volcanic eruption in history. Some key data within this dataset are the eruption date, the volcano type, location, explosivity index, data on the eruptions effects, and wheather or not a tsunami or an eathquake occurred with the eruption.

As you can see, this data is in pretty tidy shape! All the data types are the the correct and necessary type. As you can see, there are a couple of columns that only hold null values. These columns are unnecessary, so I will drop them. If we were to use the coordinates column in our analysis, I would suggest that separate that column into latitude and longnitude and change their data type to a float data type. I feel like a lot of the columns could have better names, because the columns that include the word "description" actually hold data that describes the range category of that column. So, I will want to rename some columns to fit their data better. One issue with this dataset, is that it offers no insight as to if the volcano was in an inhabited area by humans or not. Not having this data may make my analyses harder to interpret, but the information I gain from my analyses without that data will still hold value.

In [3]:
data = data.dropna(axis=1, how='all') # getting rid of columns with all null values
data = data.rename(columns={"Volcano : Deaths Description":"Vol_Deaths_Range", 
                            "Volcano : Missing Description":"Vol_Missing_Range", 
                            "Volcano : Injuries Description":"Vol_Injuries_Range", 
                            "Volcano : Damage Description":"Vol_Damage_Range(in_M$)",
                            "Volcano : Houses destroyed Description":"Vol_Houses_Destroyed_Range",
                            "Total Effects : Deaths Description":"Tot_Deaths_Range", 
                            "Total Effects : Missing Description":"Tot_Missing_Range",
                            "Total Effects : Injuries Description":"Tot_Injuries_Range",
                            "Total Effects : Damages in million Dollars":"Tot_Damage(in_M$)",
                            "Total Effects : Damage Description":"Tot_Damage_Range(in_M$)", 
                            "Total Effects : Houses Destroyed Description":"Tot_Houses_Destroyed_Range",  
                            "Total Effects : Houses Damaged Description":"Tot_Houses_Damaged_Range",
                            "Volcano : Deaths":"Vol_Deaths", 
                            "Volcano : Missing":"Vol_Missing", 
                            "Volcano : Injuries":"Vol_Injuries", 
                            "Volcano : Damage (in M$)":"Vol_Damage(in_M$)",
                            "Volcano : Houses destroyed":"Vol_Houses_Destroyed",
                            "Total Effects : Deaths":"Tot_Deaths", 
                            "Total Effects : Missing":"Tot_Missing",
                            "Total Effects : Injuries":"Tot_Injuries", 
                            "Total Effects : Houses Destroyed":"Tot_Houses_Destroyed",  
                            "Total Effects : Houses Damaged":"Total Effects : Houses Damaged Range"})
In [4]:
#https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50375985/pandas-add-column-with-value-based-on-condition-based-on-other-columns

data.assign(Earthquake_Tsunami=lambda x: x['Flag Tsunami'])
data.loc[data['Flag Tsunami'] == "Tsunami",'Earthquake_Tsunami'] = 'Tsunami'
data.loc[data['Flag Earthquake'] == "Earthquake",'Earthquake_Tsunami'] = 'Earthquake'
data.loc[(data['Flag Earthquake'] == "Earthquake") & (data['Flag Tsunami'] == "Tsunami"),'Earthquake_Tsunami'] = 'both'
data.loc[(data['Flag Earthquake'] != "Earthquake") & (data['Flag Tsunami'] != "Tsunami"),'Earthquake_Tsunami'] = 'none'

Above, I am adding a new column to the dataframe that includes both the tsunami and earthquake data so I can better use this data in my prediction model

In [5]:
# https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50375985/pandas-add-column-with-value-based-on-condition-based-on-other-columns
data['current'] = np.where(data['Year']>=1900, 'yes', 'no')

Above I am adding a current column, to divide my dataset into current and pervious eruptions so I can preform a hypothesis test on these categories later.

In [6]:
data = data[['Year', 'Flag Tsunami', 'Flag Earthquake', 'Country', 'Elevation', 'Volcano Type', 'Volcanic Explosivity Index', 'Vol_Deaths', 'Vol_Deaths_Range', 'Tot_Deaths', 'Tot_Deaths_Range', 'Earthquake_Tsunami', 'current']]

In [7]:
# Got this from 
# https://scales.arabpsychology.com/stats/how-to-add-a-count-column-to-a-pandas-dataframe/
data['vol_type_erupt_count'] = data.groupby('Volcano Type')['Year'].transform('count')

Statistical Analysis and Interpretation¶

In [8]:
VolCount = data[['Volcano Type', "Country"]].groupby(['Volcano Type',]).nunique()
alt.Chart(VolCount, title="The Number of Countries Each Volcano Type is in").mark_line().encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcano Type:N"),
    y=alt.Y("Country:Q", title="Number of Countries")
).properties(
    height=200,
    width=600
)
Out[8]:

This is a line graph of the number of countries each type of volcano has a recorded eruption in. I was expecting there to be volcanoes that had more countries that they have had eruptions in based on their nature, for example, the subglacial volcano is only going to be in places where there are glaciers. I was very surprised to see that stratovolcanoes are in over 35 different countries and that the volcano types are not more evely distributed around the world. The second most common volcano is the caldera, in about 15 different countries. This data may make it hard to differentiate data when we are later making a prediction model.

In [9]:
alt.Chart(data, title="The Number of Deaths Per Explosivity Index").mark_area().encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcanic Explosivity Index"),
    y=alt.Y("mean(Vol_Deaths):Q", title="Mean Deaths")
).properties(
    height=200,
    width=600
)
Out[9]:

This area plot of the volcanoes data set shows the relationship between the mean number of deaths and explosivity index. This graph behaves exactly as I thought it would. We don't see any real increase in deaths until we reach an explosivity index of 2.0. From that point on, the mean amount of deaths increases exponentially at each explosivity index. Going from around 0 at 2.0, to about 300-400 at 3.0, to around 1000 at 4.0, to around 2200 at 5.0, to 5500 at 6.0, to 11000 at 7.0. This shows that there is a correlation between the amount of deaths that result of a volcanic eruption and the volcanic explosivity index of that eruption.

In [12]:
vols = data.copy()
vols = deaths[(deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Caldera') | (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Tuff cone') | 
              (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Stratovolcano') | (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Shield volcano') | 
              (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Complex volcano') | (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Pyroclastic shield') |
              (deaths['Volcano Type'] == 'Maar')]

volg = vols[['Country', 'Volcano Type', 'Vol_Deaths']].groupby(['Country', 'Volcano Type']).mean()
vold = pd.DataFrame(volg)
vold = vold[vold.Vol_Deaths > 5]

DC = alt.Chart(vold, title="The Number of Deaths Per Country").mark_bar().encode(
    x=alt.X("Country:N"),
    y=alt.Y("Vol_Deaths:Q", title="Mean Deaths").scale(domain=(0,3000), clamp=True),
    color =alt.Color('Volcano Type:N')
).properties(
    height=400,
    width=1000
)

DCtext=alt.Chart(vold).mark_text(align='center', baseline='bottom', fontSize=12).encode(
    x=alt.X("Country:N"),
    y=alt.Y("Vol_Deaths:Q"),
    color=alt.Color('Volcano Type:N', legend=None, scale=alt.Scale(range=['black'])),
    text=alt.Text("Vol_Deaths:Q",format='.1f')
)

alt.layer(DC, DCtext).resolve_scale(color='independent')

# Text adjustment help from: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61657741/altair-bar-chart-label-placement-and-formating
Out[12]:

This bar chart of the countries volcanoes data set shows the distributions of mean deaths per country, separated by the volcano type. This visualization really helps me see what countries have the highest mean deaths and it tells me which volcanoes produced them in each country. This dataset overwhelmingly shows that stratovolcanoes are the top contributors for high volume deaths, folloed by calderas, complex volcanoes, maars, shield volcanoes, tuff cones, and finally pyroclastic shields. This bar chart is scaled so we can see the separations of the volcano types, but they have labels, so we can see the extemely high bars' data. It is interesting to note, that the countries with the highest experience with volcanoes, meaning the countries with the most volcanic eruptions recorded, have lower death counts than countries with less experience. This may show that countries with higher experience may be better at dealing with volcanic eruptions than less experienced ones. There are significant size differences between countries volcano types deaths means, so this shows me that these variables can be used as predictors in my model.

In [13]:
datav = data[data['vol_type_erupt_count'] > 2]
bar = alt.Chart(data, title="Volcano Type Versus Deaths: After Effects").mark_bar().encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcano Type:N"),
    y=alt.Y("mean(Tot_Deaths):Q").scale(domain=(0,3000), clamp=True).stack(None),
    color=alt.Color("Earthquake_Tsunami:N"),
    order=alt.Order(
      # Sort the segments of the bars by this field
      'mean(Tot_Deaths):Q',
      sort='descending'
    )
).properties(
    height=500,
    width=800
)

EEtext=alt.Chart(data).mark_text(align='left', baseline='bottom', dx=10, fontSize=12).encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcano Type:N"),
    y=alt.Y("mean(Tot_Deaths):Q"),
    color=alt.Color("Earthquake_Tsunami:N", legend=None),
    text=alt.Text("count(Year):Q",format='.1f')
)

EDtext=alt.Chart(data).mark_text(align='right', baseline='bottom', fontSize=13).encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcano Type:N"),
    y=alt.Y("mean(Tot_Deaths):Q"),
    color=alt.Color("Earthquake_Tsunami:N", legend=None),
    text=alt.Text("mean(Tot_Deaths):Q",format='.1f')
)

#bar + EEtext + EDtext
alt.layer(bar, EEtext, EDtext).resolve_scale(color='independent')
# ordering of segments: https://altair-viz.github.io/gallery/stacked_bar_chart_sorted_segments.html
Out[13]:

This bar plot of the volcanos dataset shows the distributions of deaths across the different types of volcanoes in the data, separated by wheteher or not a tsunami or earthquake happened afterwards. Out of the 14 volcano types presented, 10 show to have had tsunamis or earthquakes happen afterward. Out of those 10 types, 7 show that eruptions that have tsunamis or earthquakes happen with the eruption have higher mean deaths happen than eruptions by themselves. Out of tsunamis, earthquakes, or both, earthquakes tend to be the natural disaster that has the lower amounts of deaths. I expected that having both natural disasters happen would cause the most deaths, in actuality, there are instances of both having the most deaths occur from different volcano types. The shield volcano is the only volcano type to produce more deaths from an eruption by itself, than having tsunamis or earthquakes occur with it. Overall, the means of each volcano type's natural disaster is different from all the others and shows that we could use the earthquake_tsunami column as a predictor to predict the range of deaths that occur from a volcanic eruption.

In [14]:
alt.Chart(data, title="Explosivity Indexes of Different Volcano Types").mark_boxplot().encode(
    x=alt.X("Volcanic Explosivity Index:Q", title="Explosivity Index"),
    y=alt.Y("Volcano Type:N")
).properties(
    height=400,
    width=600
)
Out[14]:

This boxplot of the volcanoes data set shows the distributions of volcanic explosivity index across the different types of volcanoes in the data. Submarine volcanoes, tuff cones, volcanic fields,subglacial volcanoes, pyroclastic cones, maars, and crater rows do not have any data shown in the visualization, so that means that there is only 1 piece of data on them in the dataset. This shows that these types of volcanoes are either uncommon, or that they do not erupt very often, with my dataset, I cannot exactly tell which statement is true, but if it is that they do not erupt often, it can be concidered safe to live by one. Compound volcanoes and fizzure vents have the lowest median of a 1.5 explosivity index. Followed by calderas, complex volcanoes, lava domes, and shield volcanoes, which have a median explosivity index of 2.0. Next are stratovolcanoes and pyroclastic shields with a median of 3.0. Finally cinder cones have the highest median of a 3.5 explosivity index. Cinder cones and compound volcanoes have the smallest interquartile ranges, suggesting that their eruptions explosivity indexes stay pretty consistent. Calderas, pyroclastic shields, and lava domes have the highest IQR range, suggesting that their eruptions explosivity indexes varry a lot and it is hard to predict what explosivity index could occur from their eruptions. No volcano has a IQR that is completely separated from all the other volcanoes, suggesting that there is not a statistically significant difference in their eruptions explosivity indexes to each other. However, there are some volcanoes that have a statistically significant difference in their eruptions explosivity indexes, such as cinder cones, complex volcanoes, and compound volcanoes, which their indexes decreases respectively. Another example is fizzure vents and pyroclastic shields, where pyroclastic shields have a higher explosivity index. Overall the graph suggests that cinder cones, pyroclastic shields, stratovolcanoes, and sometimes calderas tend to have higher explosivity indexes, while compound volcanoes and fizzure vents have lower explosivity indexes.

In [15]:
alt.Chart(data, title="Deaths Per Explosivity Index").mark_boxplot().encode(
    x=alt.X("Tot_Deaths:Q", title="Deaths").scale(domain=(0,4500), clamp=True),
    y=alt.Y("Volcanic Explosivity Index:N")
    #color=alt.Color("gender", legend=None)
).properties(
    height=200,
    width= 1000
)
Out[15]:

This boxplot of the volcano data set shows the distributions of deaths that occured from each volcanic explosivity index. Volcanoes with explosivity indexes of 0-3 relatively have the same medians of death, not really showing very much significant statistical difference between them. Volcanoes with explosivity index of 3 have a slightly higher interquartile range of 68, which means that these volcanoes with explosivity index of 3 have slightly higher variation of data and have slighly higher data points than the other lower explosivity indexes, which could be a slight differentiation point between the two. Volcanoes with explosivity indexes 4-6 have bigger interquartile ranges, which exponentially increase from explosivity index 4, with the smallest range, to index 5, then 6 with the largest interquartile range. Large interquartile ranges typically mean that there is a large spread within the data, which can show that their median may not be as accurate. The median deaths for each volcanic explosivity index increases from 4-6, with index 4 having a median of 74, 5 with 864, and 6 with 2000. Index 5's interquartile range overlaps with the interquartile ranges and the medians of indexes 4 and 6, which means that these ranges are not easily differentiated from one another. Indexes 4 and 6 do not have overlapping interquartile ranges. Overall, the volcanic explosivity index could be used to determine the number of deaths that result from a volcanic eruption, but there may be some accuracy issues due to the overlapping interquartile ranges of indexes 4-6 and the similar medians of indexes 0-3 which makes differentiation of the categories. It is significant though, that the medians of death increases with each volcanic index.

In [16]:
DeathExCorr = data['Volcanic Explosivity Index'].corr(data['Tot_Deaths'])
DeathExCorr
Out[16]:
0.35306942345479864

As shown above, there is a slight positive correlation between the number of deaths that occur from volcanic eruptions and each volcanic index. A reason why these categories may be inacurate may be due to the fact that there are more people living in the world today that could be effected by a volcanic eruption, than there were in historic eruptuions

In [17]:
catCount = data[['Tot_Deaths_Range', "Volcanic Explosivity Index"]].groupby(['Tot_Deaths_Range',]).count()
catCount
Out[17]:
Volcanic Explosivity Index
Tot_Deaths_Range
Few (~1 to 50 deaths) 314
Many (~101 to 1000 deaths) 79
Some (~51 to 100 deaths) 39
Very Many (~1001 or more deaths) 40

As shown above, there is a significant rare class problem. There is significantly more data for the "Few (~1 to 50 deaths)" with 314 data points, compared to the other 3 categories with less than 80 data points. This here tells me that I will probably have to use a method of oversampling in order to use this data to predict a model.

In [18]:
DeathMax = data.copy()
DeathMax = DeathMax[DeathMax['Vol_Deaths'].notnull()]
DeathMax = DeathMax[DeathMax.Year > 1500]

meanDeaths = DeathMax[['Year', 'Vol_Deaths']].groupby('Year').mean()
mYrDeaths = pd.DataFrame(meanDeaths).reset_index()

deathsYears = alt.Chart(mYrDeaths, title="Deaths Versus Year").mark_point(filled=True).encode(
    x=alt.X("Year:Q").scale(domain=(1500,2020), clamp=True),
    y=alt.Y("Vol_Deaths:Q", title="Deaths").scale(domain=(0,6000), clamp=True)
).properties(
    height=300,
    width=750
)

deathsYears + deathsYears.transform_regression('Year', 'Vol_Deaths').mark_line(color='red')
Out[18]:

This scatter plot of the meanDeaths data set shows the relationship between the mean number of deaths that occured from all the volcanic eruptions every year from 1500-2020. There appears to be a negative correlation between the two variables. As the year increases (as we move towards the future), the mean amount of deaths that occured from volcanic eruptions in a year goes down. Up until around year 1840, the data is very sparse, for years 1800-1900 there is a medium amount of data, and 1900-present has a lot of data where the majority of the data looks like it is a low amount of deaths. Adding a line of best fit to this graph further exemplified the positive correlation between the two variables, having a clear downward slope in the right direction. We can assume from this dataset, the number of deaths that occur from volanic eruptions tends to decrease over time.

In [19]:
DeathYrCorr = mYrDeaths['Vol_Deaths'].corr(mYrDeaths['Year'])
DeathYrCorr
Out[19]:
-0.2284811555952999

A negative correlation of -0.2284811555952999 isn't that strong to actually conclude that the number of deaths that occur from volanic eruptions tends to decrease over time.

In [20]:
alt.Chart(data, title="Deaths: Current Eruptions vs Past Eruptions").mark_boxplot().encode(
    x=alt.X("Tot_Deaths:Q", title="Deaths").scale(domain=(0,1600), clamp=True),
    y=alt.Y("current:N", title="Recent Eruption")
).properties(
    height=200,
    width= 750
)
Out[20]:

This boxplot of the volcano data set shows the distributions of the number of deaths that occurred from recent eruptions (1900-2020), compared to past eruptions(-4350-1899). Past eruptions has a higher median 50 deaths compared to recent eruptions median of 3. I would say that is a significant difference. Past eruptions also has a much higher interquartile range of 4-684, compared to recent eruptions that has an interquartile range of 1-21. This means that eruptions of today most likely have more consistent death rates than past eruptions. This also means that eruptions of today most likely have lower death rates than past eruptions.

Hypothesis Test¶

Null Hypothesis: The mean number of deaths from past eruptions will be equal to the mean number of deaths from current eruptions.


Alternative Hypothesis: The mean number of deaths from past eruptions will be greater than the mean number of deaths from current eruptions.
In [21]:
data[['current', "Volcanic Explosivity Index"]].groupby(['current']).count()
Out[21]:
Volcanic Explosivity Index
current
no 317
yes 342

There are more data entries for recent eruptions, so we will need to set the permutation sample size to the previous eruptions dataset size.

In [22]:
currentErupt = data[data['current'] == 'yes']
prev = data[data['current'] == 'no']


#NOTE: This time, I am doing a one-tailed permutation test, to match my Alternative Hypothesis!!!
#I expect this number to be positive, because my test is that previous eruptions have higher death counts than recent eruptions
observedDifference = prev['Tot_Deaths'].mean() - currentErupt['Tot_Deaths'].mean()

def gen_meanDiff_permTest(df, colName, sampSize):
    df = df.sample(n = df.shape[0])
    n = int(sampSize)
    s1 = df.iloc[:n, :]
    s2 = df.iloc[n:, :]
    return s1[colName].mean() - s2[colName].mean()

#setting up the permutations:
permDiffs = []
for i in range(10000):
    #NOTE: here is where I'm specifying that one of my permutation groups should be smaller than the other!
    permDiffs.append(gen_meanDiff_permTest(data, 'Tot_Deaths', prev.shape[0]))
perms = pd.DataFrame({'diffs':permDiffs})

#Plotting for visual indication of the test
alt.data_transformers.disable_max_rows() #we have more than 5k data points being considered

deathHist = alt.Chart(perms).mark_bar().encode(
    x = alt.X('diffs:Q', title='Differences in means of random permutations').bin(maxbins=25),
    y = alt.Y('count():Q')
)

deathObs = alt.Chart().mark_rule().encode(
    x = alt.X('obsDiff:Q')
).transform_calculate(
    obsDiff=str(observedDifference)
)

deathHist + deathObs
Out[22]:
In [23]:
#To calculate the p-value from the histogram, I find the number of rows meeting the testing condition
#the p-value is then a simple division:
numExtreme = perms[perms["diffs"] > observedDifference].shape[0]
numTotal = perms.shape[0]
pValue = numExtreme / numTotal
print("My p-value is " + str(pValue) + " -- we can accept the alternative hypothesis!")

My p-value is 0.0 -- we can accept the alternative hypothesis!

The permutation test suggests there is a statistically significant difference in the number of deaths that occurred from previous eruptions and recent eruotions, since the observed p-value was 0. Since we were testing for a previous eruptions to have a higher mean of deaths, the p-value that would show the null hypothesis would be on the right side of the line, to which there were no values, so our alternative hypothesis was accepted. I was expecting this because there is better technology and knowledge available to predict and warn people about volcanic eruptions, and hence, there should be less people dying from volcanic eruptions today, than there were in the past. The true correlation of deaths and year was -0.2794972119063675, which is lower than 0 and lower than -0.5, which means there is a loosely negative correlation, meaniing that as the years increase, the number of deaths decrease. The p-value being 0 shows that this correlation is statistically significant, and I would argue to say that this correlation is also practically significant because the correlation doesn't take into concideration of whether or not the volcano happened in a populated area or not. In all, our hypothesis test shows that the fact that more deaths occurred from past eruptions is not up to random chance, it was satistically proven that more deaths did occur in the past.

Statistical Modeling with Random Forest¶

In [24]:
#Dropping all the nulls in my predictors and target so I can build my model
data.dropna(subset=['Volcanic Explosivity Index'], inplace=True)
data.dropna(subset=['Volcano Type'], inplace=True)
data.dropna(subset=['Country'], inplace=True)
data.dropna(subset=['Elevation'], inplace=True)

data.dropna(subset=['Tot_Deaths_Range'], inplace=True)
data = data[((data['Country']) != 'Pacific Ocean') & (data.Year > 1900)]
In [25]:
# I got all of the over sampling data code from:
# https://machinelearningmastery.com/random-oversampling-and-undersampling-for-imbalanced-classification/
# pip install imbalanced-learn
import imblearn
from imblearn.over_sampling import RandomOverSampler
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

target = "Tot_Deaths_Range" # Our target variable
#predictors = ["Volcanic Explosivity Index", "Earthquake_Tsunami", "Volcano Type", "Country", "Year", "Pop_Density"] # A list of predictors
predictors = ["Volcanic Explosivity Index", "Volcano Type", "Country", "Earthquake_Tsunami", "Elevation"] # A list of predictors


X = pd.get_dummies(data[predictors])
y = data[target]

oversampler = RandomOverSampler(sampling_strategy='auto', random_state=10) # 30
X, y = oversampler.fit_resample(X, y)
In [26]:
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, 
    y, 
    test_size=0.4, 
    random_state=30)

decision_tree_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 25, max_depth=10, max_leaf_nodes= 20, min_samples_split=2) #10, 20
decision_tree_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[26]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=10, max_leaf_nodes=20, random_state=25)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=10, max_leaf_nodes=20, random_state=25)
In [27]:
plt.figure(figsize=(30,20))
plot_tree(decision_tree_model, filled='true', feature_names=decision_tree_model.feature_names_in_, class_names=decision_tree_model.classes_, proportion=True, fontsize = 11)
plt.show()

This decision tree begins by splitting at the volcanic explosivity index of the volcanic eruption. The left side of the tree identifies the lower explosivity eruptions and has pretty good prediction of predicting the lower death categories. The tree runs into some issues by trying to predict the some category with the very many category. It tries to come up with a solution to predict it by bringing in the countries which helps it out a little bit with categories very many, some, and few respecively, but it has a preference of very many. The right side of the tree identifies the categories many, few, and some respectively. The tree suggests that that some predictors are better than others at predicting the death category. One such predictor is the population density, which is located at the root node and throughout the tree. It is followed by the volcanic explosivity index, country, Tsunami_Earthquake, and volcano type. All, but one, leaf nodes have gini indexes of 0.0, showing that they are very confident in their categorizations. So, this decision tree shows that this can be a good model to use on a random forest.

In [28]:
rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state = 25, max_depth=10, max_leaf_nodes= 20, min_samples_split=2) #60, 10, 5
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, 
    y, 
    test_size=0.4, #.8
    random_state=30) #15

rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

categories = rf.classes_
predictions = rf.predict(X_test)
probabilities = rf.predict_proba(X_test)
probabilities = pd.DataFrame(probabilities, 
                    columns=categories)
probabilities

319 rows × 4 columns

A good table of predictions would have a 0/1 split and a bad table would have a .5/.5 split. My table of predictions for my ranom forest classifier shows good predictability for my model. In the table above, there are 6 predictions that have over a 75% prediction, and 2 with around 64%, which show very high predictability. There's also 2 predictions that have pretty even splits between 2 or more columns, which is not good for my model, because it also means that the model isn't entirely confident in that prediction. Overall, I think this model will be an accurate one, which we will validate in the next few steps.

In [31]:
# Calculate confusion matrix and transform data
conf_mat = confusion_matrix(y_test,predictions)
conf_mat = pd.DataFrame(conf_mat,index=categories,columns=categories)
conf_mat = conf_mat.melt(ignore_index=False).reset_index()
# Create heatmap
heatmap = alt.Chart(conf_mat, title="Confusion Matrix").mark_rect().encode(
    x=alt.X("variable:N", sort=['Few (~1 to 50 deaths)','Some (~51 to 100 deaths)','Many (~101 to 1000 deaths)','Very Many (~1001 or more deaths)']).title("Predicted Response"),
    y=alt.Y("index:N", sort=['Few (~1 to 50 deaths)','Some (~51 to 100 deaths)','Many (~101 to 1000 deaths)','Very Many (~1001 or more deaths)']).title("True Response"),
    color=alt.Color("value:Q", legend=None).scale(scheme="cividis")
).properties(
    width=400,
    height=400
)
# Add text labels for numbers
text = heatmap.mark_text(baseline="middle").encode(
    text=alt.Text("value:Q"),
     color=alt.condition(
        (alt.datum['value'] < 30),
        alt.value('white'),
        alt.value('black')
    ),
    size=alt.value(50)
)

heatmap + text
Out[31]:

This confusion matrix shows all of the predicted responses from our model compared to the correct categories that come from our testing data. A good confusion matrix would have all of the responses in the diagonal from the top left corner to the bottom right. My confusion matrix does a very good job at that. My confusion matrix shows that my model predicted the right category 260 times out of 319, and predicted wrong only 59 times out of 319 times. This shows that my model is very accurate, but still predicts wrong sometimes. This matrix also shoes me that the model is a little less confident in predicting the "Few (~1 to 50 deaths)" category because it has a lot of missed predictions in the same row. Overall, this confusion matrix shows that my model is very accurate.

In [32]:
# found idxmax from: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/29919306/find-the-column-name-which-has-the-maximum-value-for-each-row
pred = probabilities.copy()
pred['MaxPred'] = pred.max(axis=1)

predHist = alt.Chart(pred, title='Prediction Percentage Distribution').mark_bar().encode(
    x = alt.X('MaxPred:Q', title='Prediction Percentage').bin(maxbins=10),
    y = alt.Y('count():Q')
).properties(
    height=400,
    width=300
)
predHist
Out[32]:

This histogram shows the prediction percentages of each of the predicted categories. In a perfect world, you would want all of your prediction percentages to be as high as possible. In my histogram, my graph shows that my model only has around 163 predictions of 20%-49% which shows that the model is pretty confused. There are a high count of rows, around 57, that shows a 50% chance of being confused, which isn't great, but it is acceptable because there are 4 categories and the percentages could be split up to be 16% each for the other categories, which could in turn be significant. 60% is where the model starts to show some probability. There are around 59 counts of predictions that are between 60%-69%, 60 counts of 70%-79%, and around 87 counts of 80%-89%. Altogether, this counts for 110/319 probabilities that show good confidence. Including 50%-59%, we have 167/319 which is a little over half of the predictions. Overall, this validation shows that my model has a good probability of miscategorizing the death range.

In [33]:
print(classification_report(y_test, predictions))

                                  precision    recall  f1-score   support

           Few (~1 to 50 deaths)       0.91      0.62      0.74        80
      Many (~101 to 1000 deaths)       0.82      0.87      0.84        83
        Some (~51 to 100 deaths)       0.77      0.84      0.80        79
Very Many (~1001 or more deaths)       0.80      0.94      0.86        77

                        accuracy                           0.82       319
                       macro avg       0.82      0.82      0.81       319
                    weighted avg       0.82      0.82      0.81       319

In the classification report shown, you can see that our model is 82% accurate. The precision scores all showed my model to be an accurate model with scores of 77% and above, which shows that when my model predicts a category, it is correct 77% of the time. My model has recall scores of above 84%, except for the "Few (1 to 50 deaths)", which is 62%, which means that I am only correct at predicting that category 62% of the time. This means that my model is very good at predicting the categories, but is a little confused when predicting the "Few (1 to 50 deaths)" category. The accuracy score was very good at an 82%, which is a good accuracy overall. Overall, these scores indicate that my model is very good at predicting the range of deaths that will occur from an eruption, but can get a little confused when predicting the "Few (~1 to 50 deaths)" category.

In [34]:
# I got the sort from: github.com/vega/altair/issues/1137
heatmap2 = alt.Chart(data, title="Deaths Range Per Explosivity Index").mark_rect().encode(
    x=alt.X("Tot_Deaths_Range:N", sort=['Few (~1 to 50 deaths)','Some (~51 to 100 deaths)','Many (~101 to 1000 deaths)','Very Many (~1001 or more deaths)']).title("Deaths Range"),
    y=alt.Y("Volcanic Explosivity Index").bin(maxbins=8),
    color=alt.Color("count():Q", legend=None).scale(scheme="cividis")
).properties(
    width=400,
    height=400
)

text2 = heatmap2.mark_text(baseline="middle").encode(
    text=alt.Text("count():Q"),
     color=alt.condition(
        (alt.datum['count():Q'] < 30),
        alt.value('white'),
        alt.value('black')
    ),
    size=alt.value(50)
)
heatmap2 + text2
Out[34]:

Lastly, here is a heatmap of the counts of the volcanic explosivity index versus the deaths range of volcanic explosions. Excluding the "Few (1 to 50 deaths)" category, we can see that as the range of deaths goes up, the counts of higher explosivity indexes typically is higher than the lower death ranges, and the counts of lower indexes goes is higher for smaller death ranges. This shows the generic correlation of explosivity index versus death that we explored previously. My model showed that it struggled with predicting the "Few (1 to 50 deaths)" category, and I think this model supports my hypothesis that it has to do with the population density around a volcano, and that is the missing predictor in our model. As we can see, there are high counts of few deaths for the explosity indexes 5 and 6, I hypothesize that this is due to there not being many people around the volcano to be affected by the natural disaster, which would explain why there would be few deaths that occurred from the eruption.

Conclusion¶

In the end, there were clear patterns in the volcanoes dataset that allowed us to predict the range of deaths that will occur from volcanic eruptions. Overall the most predictive predictor was the volcanic explosivity index followed by the volcano evelation, earthquakes and tsunamis, and the country of the eruption. I was also able to prove my hypothesis through hypothesis testing, that it was statistically significant that, and not up to random chance, that more deaths resulted from volcanic eruptions in the past then they do today. Which signaled that we have learned more about volcanoes and have learned how to better deal with them. Through my exploritory data analysis, I was able to pick up on the patterns that the volcanic explosivity index, volcano type, country, and the year all correlated with the number of deaths that resulted from that eruption, but they never seemed fully accurate, for their data sometimes varried. This is when I hypothesized that there could be another predictor that could help with the correlations. I hypothesized that the missing data piece was the population density surrounding the volcano, because an eruption with an explosivity index could occur in the middle of nowhere with nobody around to be affected by it. This is why I believe my accuracy in predicting the few deaths category is messed up, because without the population density factor going into concideration, my model will predict eruptions of higher death counts. The only issue was, that I didn't have access to data that was that precise for the number of eruptions I needed for my random forest classifier model.

This problem was tackled within the Journal of Applied Volcanology. Their model was very similar to mine, but instead of predicting the range of deaths that resulted in a volcano, they predicted the percent chance that a person would survive a volcano. In their models the predictors they used that were similar to mine were the volcanic explosivity index, volcano type/volcano hazards, earthquakes, and tsunamis. Their dataset also held the distance away from the volcano, which was not in their dataset, and is what would be the equivalent of a population density of a distance surrounding a volcano for my model. Their findings were very similar to mine in every category. In future analysis, I would suggest that a population density surrounding the volcano be added for as many eruptions we can find. That would allow the model to become less confused when predicting the few deaths category.

Overall, even without the population density predictior, my model of a random forest classifier performed really well with an 82% accuracy rate to predict the range of deaths that would result from a volcanic eruption. In my random forest classifier, I used the data that was past the year 1900 becuase my hypothesis test stated that the number of deaths that occured before that was statistically significantly higher than the recent eruptions, which would possibly confuse my model if I kept it in. This worked really well, and I believe this makes my model more relevant to future eruptions. In future studies, I may work with the coordinates data included in the significant-volcanic-eruption-database.csv to find population densities around a volcano and if I can, I can record the population densities myself for future eruptions in order to make a better model. In all, I feel like I learned a lot about volcanoes and what makes them such a disasterous natural disaster, and I feel like my model can help predict future eruption deaths as well.

Brown, S.K., Jenkins, S.F., Sparks, R.S.J. et al. "Volcanic fatalities database: analysis of volcanic threat with distance and victim classification." Journal of Applied Volcanology, 20 Sept. 2017, https://doi.org/10.1186/s13617-017-0067-4

In [ ]:
